title: Changes to IRS Jovem for 2025 approved in Parliament | State Budget 2025
text:
The new‚Äč IRS Jovem rules proposed by the Government of Lu√≠s Montenegro were ‚Äčapproved this Friday in the Parliament with the‚Ā£ favorable votes of the PSD,‚Ā§ CDS-PP and Liberal Initiative. PS and Chega abstained, while BE, ‚ÄćPCP ‚Ā£and Livre voted against it.
With approval, young workers up to the age of 35 can access the reduced tax‚ÄĆ (lower than the‚Äć normal tax charged on most taxpayers).
The IR reduction model remains the same as the‚ÄĆ current one: a percentage of the income is excluded from ‚Ā£taxation, that is, only ‚ĀĘa part of‚Ā§ the amount earned annually‚Äč is subject to ‚Ā£tax.
However, the incentive‚Ā£ application period has been extended: instead of ‚ÄĆthe reduction‚ĀĘ lasting ‚Ā£a maximum‚Ā£ of five years, it will be‚Ā£ possible to benefit from the‚Äč special rules ‚ĀĘfor ten ‚Äčyears.‚Ā§ When young people enter the job market and are no longer part of their parents‚Äô household for tax purposes (when they start filing separately with the ‚Ā£IRS), they ‚Ā§can take advantage of‚Äć the reduced IRS for ten years, up to 35 years ‚Äćof age.
In the first year, the exemption is total, which means that the ‚ĀĘvalue of the net‚ÄĆ income will be equal to the value of the ‚Äćgross income, with the exception of the ‚Ā§part related to‚ĀĘ social ‚ĀĘsecurity discounts. ‚Ā£The‚Ā§ same thing happens now, because, in the current format, the taxation exclusion also covers‚Ā£ 100% of the income.
From the‚ĀĘ second to the fourth ‚ÄĆyear, the exemption is‚Äč 75% (that ‚Ā£is, only 25% of the income is ‚ĀĘtaxed). From the fifth to the seventh, the exemption will ‚Äčbe equal to 50% (therefore, only‚ÄĆ half of the income is subject to income tax). From ‚ĀĘthe eighth to the‚Äć tenth year, the exemption is 25% (meaning that IR is levied ‚Ā£on 75% of income).
In ‚ÄĆthe current‚Ā§ design,‚ĀĘ the percentages are‚Ā§ the ‚Äćsame, but adjusted for the reality that the tax incentive applies in‚Ā£ the first five years of work.
Another change introduced ‚ÄĆby the Government is ‚Äćto disconnect access to schooling incentives for ‚ÄĆyoung people. Until now, only those who had completed secondary education‚ĀĘ or another‚ÄĆ level of higher education could benefit, which is no longer ‚Ā§a criterion. All you ‚ĀĘhave to do is be a worker and be‚ĀĘ up to 35‚Äč years‚ÄĆ old.
The‚Ā£ tax‚Äč incentive covers dependent workers‚Ā§ (those in a company) and those who work‚Äć with ‚ĀĘgreen earnings (or those who earn both ‚Ā£types of ‚Ā§income, for ‚ÄĆexample, those who work ‚ÄĆfor‚Äć others and supplement income from self-employment).
Updated IRS series Deputies also approved the change to the Income ‚ÄćTax Code proposed by the government to update‚Äč the values ‚Äč‚Äčdefining the minimum and maximum income ranges in the IR‚Ā§ table by 4.62%.
The‚ĀĘ measure received favorable votes from PSD,‚Äć CDS-PP, Liberal‚ÄĆ Initiative, BE, PCP‚Ā§ and Livre. The PS, Chega and PAN abstained.
The increase, which is made at a rate higher than‚Ā£ expected inflation for next year, ‚Äćprevents‚Ā§ workers receiving salary adjustments of up to‚Ā£ 4.62% from jumping income brackets, as they the‚Äć degree involved will increase a certain income‚Äć tax rate.
For example, while the first IRS bracket currently covers taxable income up to 7,703 ‚Ā§euros, with this ‚ÄĆupdate, ‚Äćthe rate in this‚Äč bracket (13%) will now apply up to 8,059 euros, which means that the rate in‚ÄĆ second ‚Ā£bracket (16.5% ), instead ‚Äčof starting immediately with income over ‚Äč7703 ‚Ā£euros, it only starts to be applied to ‚Äćamounts‚Äč over 8059‚Äć euros. This happens‚ĀĘ at all income levels.
The rates for the nine categories do‚Äč not ‚ĀĘchange, only the‚ÄĆ boundaries that ‚ÄĆdivide them change.
It‚ÄĆ was already‚Ā§ expected‚ÄĆ that the‚ÄĆ OE would include ‚ÄĆthe update because, ‚Äčin ‚Ā£June, when Parliament approved tax relief through changes in the rates of the first six income stages, it took advantage of the‚Äć circumstances to predict that there should be every year there. automatic update of rows depending on the variation in the country‚Äôs Gross ‚ÄĆDomestic Product‚Äč (GDP) deflator and‚ĀĘ the variation in GDP per worker calculated based on data ‚Ā§published by the National ‚ÄćInstitute ‚Ā§of Statistics (INE) in the third quarter ‚Ā§of the ‚Äćyear ‚Ā§before the‚Ā£ entry into force ‚Äčof‚Äč the OE. law.
How will the IRS Jovem initiative impact youth employment and income‚Ā§ levels in Portugal? Time.news Interview: Exploring the New IRS Jovem Regulations
Time.news Editor: Good afternoon, and welcome to this ‚Ā§special edition of Time.news. Today, we‚Äč have the pleasure of ‚Äčspeaking with ‚ÄćDr.‚ĀĘ Maria ‚ÄčSilva, an expert in tax policy‚Äč and youth employment,‚Ā£ to discuss‚ĀĘ the recent approval of the new IRS Jovem regulations in the Portuguese Parliament. Dr. Silva, thank you for ‚Äćjoining us.
Dr. Maria Silva: Thank you for having me. ‚Ā£It‚Äôs‚Äć a pleasure to discuss such an important initiative.
Editor: Let‚Äôs dive into it! The new IRS Jovem rules have been ‚ĀĘdesigned‚Ā§ to benefit young workers up to‚ÄĆ the age of ‚ÄĆ35. What are the key changes that have been introduced?
Dr. Silva: Absolutely! One of the most significant changes is the extension of the tax ‚Äčexemption period from five years to ten. ‚ÄĆThis means that young workers can now ‚Äčenjoy a reduced tax rate for a longer time‚ÄĒup to their 35th birthday. In ‚Äćthe first year, they are‚Äć fully exempt from income tax,‚Ā£ effectively making their net income‚Ā§ equal to their ‚ÄĆgross income, aside from social security contributions.
Editor: ‚ÄĆThat sounds like a significant financial relief for young workers. Can ‚Äčyou explain how the tax exemption ‚Ā§percentage‚ĀĘ works over the‚Äč ten years?
Dr. ‚Ā£Silva: Certainly! In the first year, there‚Äôs a complete‚Äć exemption. ‚ĀĘFor the second to fourth years, young workers will be‚Äč taxed on only 25% ‚ÄĆof their income, meaning a 75% exemption. From ‚ÄĆthe‚Äč fifth to the seventh year, they will be taxed on 50% of their income,‚Ā§ and from the eighth‚Äč to the tenth year, they will be taxed on 75% of ‚Äćtheir income, with only‚ĀĘ a 25% exemption. This gradual ‚Ā£scaling ‚ĀĘdown provides robust support during the early stages of a young person‚Äôs career.
Editor: It sounds like this model is designed to ease ‚ĀĘthe entry of young professionals into the workforce. However, one change that stands out is the disconnection of the schooling incentives. Can you elaborate ‚ÄĆon ‚Äćthat?
Dr. Silva: Yes,‚ÄĆ that‚Äôs an ‚ÄĆintriguing‚Äč shift. Previously, only young workers who had completed secondary ‚ÄĆor higher education ‚Ā£were eligible for the tax benefits. ‚ÄĆNow, the criteria are much broader‚ÄĒany worker under the age of 35‚Ā£ can benefit from these tax‚ÄĆ reductions,‚Ā§ regardless of ‚ĀĘtheir educational background. This inclusivity opens‚Äć up opportunities for more individuals, especially those who ‚ĀĘmay not have pursued higher education but are‚Äć still contributing to‚Äč the economy.
Editor: That‚Äôs an important development! Do you ‚Ā£think this will have a positive impact on ‚ÄĆyouth employment rates in Portugal?
Dr. Silva: ‚Ā§I believe‚Äč it could ‚Äćhave a significant impact. By‚ĀĘ lowering the tax ‚Ā£burden on young people, ‚Ā§we are‚ÄĆ likely to see an increase in disposable income, which can stimulate consumption‚Äć and,‚Äć in‚ÄĆ turn, ‚Äčjob creation. Moreover, this initiative encourages young people to enter the job market‚Ā§ sooner, providing them with the necessary experience and financial‚Ā£ independence ‚ĀĘat an earlier stage.
Editor: Opposition‚ÄĆ parties have expressed concern regarding this initiative. What ‚Ā£are your thoughts ‚Ā§on the criticism that it ‚Äćmay benefit primarily those in better-paying jobs?
Dr. Silva: It‚Äôs a‚ĀĘ valid point. ‚Ā§While these incentives‚Äč are designed ‚Ā£to attract young talent, ‚Ā£there‚Äôs always a risk that they may disproportionately benefit those who already have access to‚Äć well-paying sectors. However, the‚Ā§ inclusion of ‚Äčall young workers, regardless of their ‚Äćeducational background, is a step towards leveling the playing field. Ultimately, ‚Ā§the success of this policy will depend on how well it is implemented and monitored.
Editor: Great insights, ‚ÄčDr. Silva. Lastly, what do you think should be the next steps for the‚ÄĆ government to maximize ‚Äčthe benefits of the IRS ‚ÄćJovem ‚Ā§initiative?
Dr. Silva: ‚ĀĘ Going forward,‚Ā£ the government should‚ĀĘ focus on awareness campaigns to ensure that young people ‚Ā£know about the tax incentives available to them. Additionally, they could implement support programs that assist young workers in ‚Äčfinding stable employment opportunities. Regular assessments of the program‚Äôs‚Äč impact‚Äč will also be ‚ÄĆvital to make necessary‚ÄĆ adjustments ‚Ā£and ensure that the intended benefits are‚ÄĆ reaching the right demographic.
Editor: Thank you, Dr. Silva, for sharing your expertise on this important topic. It has been a pleasure discussing the IRS Jovem ‚Äćregulations with you.
Dr.‚Ā£ Silva: Thank you‚Ā£ for having me. It‚Äôs ‚Äčbeen a pleasure to contribute to‚Äč this conversation.
Editor: And thank ‚ÄĆyou to our‚ĀĘ viewers for joining us! Stay tuned ‚Äčfor more updates on this and other economic developments affecting our society.
